sentences.text: After what?
I remember that-- I remember that it got worse, very quickly.
Not very-- the bombs didn't fly, you know, and the gas didn't come.
But what happened one day, I was going to the center, which is just a block or two from my house.
The center is very small now.
I thought it was much bigger.
When I went back, I realized it shrunk.
It was like not even half what I thought it was.
Because I was a little girl.
When you're a little girl, things seem bigger.
And I couldn't believe how small this little square.
In my mind's eye, it was bigger.
But that little square was the center, whatever it was.
There wasn't like a square, like a small square.
And that's where all of a sudden I come, and there I saw the Nazis with the boots, arriving in a Jeep.
And they came out.
Until today, I have fright in me when I think of it.
That first day, when they walked out of a Jeep, open Jeep, what had no doors.
And they wore high boots, with sticks they had.
And they must have been either the Gestapo or the ones that were putting fright into Jewish people.
Exactly what their capacity was-- but they looked awful, they acted awful.
They acted-- they went and they lined up men, right in the middle.
And they sheared the beards off.
I saw a man, and he cut off his beard, a half like this and half-- to make fun and to make ridicule the Jewish orthodox men, the men that I had such respect for before the war.
This was the elders, the people who had the right to have respect for them.
And they made the opposite.
They made ridicule from the Jew.
They made him into a nothing, unhuman.
They made them to look, the Jewish people, like they were-- the most orthodox they picked.
And it was terrible.
And I want to cry when I think of it, even today, so many years.
And it brings tears to my eyes, when I think what they did.
And they went, and they burned the shul down.
And I saw it in flames.
Until today, I see in my mind's eye the shul burning.
And I see it with flames going up.
And I think of how [NON-ENGLISH] didn't stop the flame.
They went straight to heaven.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they burned down the beis medrash, which was built from wood.
It was a very tall structure.
It must have stood there how many, who knows, maybe more than 100, I don't know.
Who knows how long it was built.
It was a very tall beis medrash.
And that's where the men learned, Gemara and everything.
The house of study.
They burned it.
Until today, there's nothing there, because I went back.
And it's an empty spot, like a hole in the ground.
There's nothing.
You would never believe that once there stood a beis medrash.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The shul, I was told by people, was beautiful.
And it's shown in one of the paintings in the book.
It was inside, I was told, it was a beautiful shul.
And it was typically built with a bimah.
And the men sat downstairs, all surrounding the bimah around.
And the women sat upstairs.
And I remember sitting on shabbos, going either with my grandmother or my mother to sit with all the ladies came from the town, to sit.
And that shul was burned when they arrived.
It was destroyed.
The Jewish people should have nothing-- it was like the beginning of the destruction of all the villages like this.
This what happened to every village.
No, I was alone.
I was just where I used to go by myself.
I would go anywhere I wanted.
I would play.
I wasn't in school yet, so I would go there or go here.
I didn't have to-- you know, I was old enough to go within blocks or even a half an hour.
I was very bright.
I could go anyplace I wanted.
And I didn't have to be accompanied because there was no-- my mother didn't have to be afraid.
I was about six and a half when this happened.
Because my mother-- I didn't remember.
If somebody said to me, when you were born, I didn't know.
But what happened was one day, when we were already in Przemysl on the Russian side, we went to visit a lady, a friend of my mother's.
And the friend asked her, how old is Friedela?
And that thing stuck in me.
And my mother answered Friedela is just turned six and a half.
And this is what-- I don't remember which year I was born.
But because of that, I could make out papers later.
Because there was no way, I didn't know.
But I remembered my mother said Friedela [YIDDISH].
In Jewish she said to the lady, because she spoke Jewish.
And from this is what I knew, because everybody knows when the war broke out.
So that's how I knew exactly, to the month, how old I was, because of what my mother said.
And I was listening.
I didn't listen to the other conversation, but for that, suddenly I perked up.
And I suddenly heard they were talking about me.
And when they said Friedel, my mind sort of jumped up.
And I said, oh, so I'm six.
That's how I later kept exactly the date.
That's because of what my mother said, OK.
So that's how old.
Six and a half when I saw these terrible things happening in our village.
I didn't say much.
I wasn't much of a talker.
I wasn't much of a [YIDDISH].
Maybe I said something, maybe.
I don't remember what I said or what I didn't.
That I don't know remember.
I just knew that that was my very first terrible memories, witnessing the Germans-- the Nazis, I mean-- coming into our village, peaceful existence.
They disrupted my life from that day on.
That was the beginning of the terrible things that I had to go through, the very beginning.
After that, things got worse.
People-- I didn't even remember that people were told to go.
I thought that-- I didn't know that the Germans told the Jews to leave.
I thought that-- Because my grandmother stayed the whole war.
And my mother left the following year, the following spring, I believe.
Because I remember according to the weather, it wasn't that cold.
And we didn't go that year, like other people left that same year.
But some people left right away.
And many men left.
Because when the men saw what they did to the men-- all the men with the beards, since my grandfather wore a beard-- he left right away.
He left to the side of the Russian side.
And my grandmother didn't want to leave.
She wanted to stay.
She was very obstinate.
She said she's not going.
And my grandfather left.
And other men left, and other families left.
But some remained.
And then, one day, my mother said we're going to the Russian side.
And we took whatever belonging, what can you carry on your shoulders when you leave a home.
You disrupt a home.
You have furniture, you have everything.
You have normal things.
You have even some silver.
My mother had silver.
My mother had beautiful things in the house.
It wasn't a big house, but it was beautiful furnished.
She had-- I have to describe the velvet spread she had.
They were one of a kind, I have never seen since.
There were velvet green with red roses on the side, like cabbage roses.
And whenever I looked at that, that was my-- I loved the color and the feel.
They were soft, and she had this on the beds.
And I remember those spreads.
And she had the nice table and chairs.
And everything was normal.
She had beautiful armoires standing with-- I think they were custom built.
And how do you leave all this?
Suddenly you have to take something on the back what you can carry and leave.
It's a terrible feeling.
You don't know what should-- if you have to go, anybody has to go out of a home, what should I take?
What should I leave?
It's a terrible thing to go through.
But my mother did-- and she was alone.
You have to realize, she was all alone.
And how do you take two children, you know, when they start crying, you're hungry, I'm thirsty.
How do you-- just the idea of taking two children on the road, they have to cross the River San to the Russian side.
I don't know if you realize what hardship this was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's terrible to go through this.
I mean, you had to walk and you had to-- we had to walk to a certain point, and then I think she hired a-- I remember I walked and walked.
And I got sleepy and thirsty.
And my mother didn't have water.
And I remember she took something, she had something with her, something very precious.
A little, like a basket made from straw, but a bottle was inside.
And there was something like cherries with liqueur or something.
And she gave us to drink a sip of that.
And I remember she didn't have water, but says drink that.
And that's what I remember from the journey, not too much more.
I remember we had to--
Delicious, like I never tasted anything since.
I wish I could taste this.
It was something that she had, like you keep something very precious for [YIDDISH].
Like I have a full-- I have liqueur and everything.
But this, she had one bottle of this, and she took that.
And this was so delicious, with cherries or something.
I don't know, there was some kind of fruit.
And she gave me a sip of that, and that calmed me.
And I remember, I was a little girl.
I wanted a drink, and I was tired.
And I realized the abnormality, the things that were happening-- you know, a child gets very annoyed when you disrupt the normal life, you know?
And my mother--
Anyway, we finally did arrive to Przemysl, eventually, which was-- yeah, we had to cross a river, the dividing line.
I remember this distinctly.

I remember as we crossed this body of water-- and I don't know where it was where we crossed, because I don't-- but I remember the Germans were standing with guns and poking at what my mother had, the belongings.
She took something maybe to cover herself.
Imagine how terrible this was.
And we had to go through like a wooden-- like, I don't know.
It wasn't a bridge.
It was like a floating wooden-- you know, to get us through this body of water.
I don't know how you say that in English.
It was something that floated, but you--
A raft.
Like a wooden raft.
And we had to cross this.
And the Germans supervised this raft, until we got to the other side.
We could still go, they told us to go.
They wanted to get rid of the Jews.
But I wish they'd all gone.
But, anyway, so my mother was there.
And I remember standing, and the men having guns.
And they poked into to see-- what they were looking, I don't know.
But they were with the guns.
When I saw those guns, I got frightened, with the Germans.
Because I already had that terrible memory of the Nazis.
And these soldiers were poking and searching if we didn't have whatever they didn't want us to take.
That's the memory I remember.
But then we crossed.
And we found ourselves in Przemysl, which was safe.
Because the Russians didn't bother the Jewish people.
Some normalcy was-- we weren't in our own home.
We had to find inferior quality living space, but we found.
Eventually, we lived in a house, which was first time I ever lived on a-- you know, where the house was more than one story.
It was a very old house.
Przemysl is a very old city.
And this house was not new.
It was, you know, the best that my mother could afford at the time.
And then I remember that one of my mother's sisters moved into that house.
So I had a little cousin.
The only cousin I ever had before the war, and he was killed in the war.
He lived in that house with my aunt and her husband.
And my sisters, of course, joined, my two sisters.
Because my grandparents also came from where they lived to the Russian side.
They were also on the German side.
They also had to leave their house and their business and everything they had.
And they had to come-- and they came to Przemysl.
Yes, after some period, right.
And we were reunited.
And I was so happy to have her.
She was such a beautiful child.
And I felt so good having my sister.
And my little sister and my grandparents were there.
And I would go-- I could go every day if I wanted to see the other grandparents and aunts and uncles.
And they were a big help to my mother.
When my mother had to, you know, scrounge whatever she needed, they tried to help her, any way they could.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had an uncle.
My grandmother had a brother living in Przemysl.
I remember they lived there too, they lived there.
So, anyway, I remember Przemysl was a very, very, very old city.
In 1966, it celebrated its 1,000th year as a city.
And it was a famed-- Jewish people lived there for hundreds of years.
Once a king lived there.
And the River San ran through it.
It was the strategic river where the Russian made the border between-- the Russians were on one side.
Wherever the River San ran through in Galicia, that's where the Russian took one side, and the German made up the other side.
So once you crossed the River San, wherever it ran, you were on either the Russian or the German side.
So we were now on the Russian side, which was better.
We lived there one year.
And then-- What was it like for that year for you?
It was fine.
It was OK.
It wasn't normal, but it was still bearable.
It was bearable, because, you know, when you have your family and, you know.
And we still had hope.
My mother still had hope maybe she will get permission from the consulate.
She went there every day to see if she could go out from Poland.
So there was still hope.
You know, the hope was still there.
But we had no idea what's waiting for us.
We had-- nobody had an idea how terrible.
It's going to get worse and worse.
She stayed in the village, but she moved into the center where the-- the few Jewish people that remained, she moved closer to them.
She didn't stay among the goyim.
Because before, her house was situated among-- there were very few Jews.
And they were scattered, but not too many.
Next to her house, there were no Jews.
So that she lived, so to speak, among the non-Jewish people.
So she moved closer the Jewish people.
She was alone.
Her husband went to Lemberg, and he died there.
We found out.
One day my mother mentioned that somebody came with the news that he died, maybe from starvation, maybe from I don't know what from.
From heart?
Who knows?
Because he couldn't just function.
He couldn't function without my grandmother took care all his-- This was during the time that you were in Przemysl you found this out?
Yes, and he died.
He died.
Poor.
Who knows where they buried him.
But he was still buried.
But you know, he wasn't made into ashes at least.
He was buried somewhere.
His grave, I have no idea.
And it was sad, but I didn't understand because I was a child.
And I didn't fully comprehend the sadness of it.
Now I realize how sad this all was.
And then, one day, my mother said, things got-- the Russians were catching people off the streets, and they put them into cars, into all kinds of trucks and send them to Siberia.
One day she said, they almost caught me in the street.
And I-- this is something new.
I didn't-- this is something-- we didn't expect that.
My mother thought this was the worst thing, to be sent off Siberia.
I don't know whether with us or without us.
I don't know.
It was terrible, scary.
And she was literally afraid to walk on the street, because they were catching people off the streets and sending-- and those were the lucky ones.
Who should have known they were going to be the lucky ones.
The ones who were sent to Siberia, some of them survived, many survived.
Comparing to the ones that were left in Poland, many survived.
Entire families.
I want to tell you something.
One family, they were not-- I didn't know it, but I was told by somebody.
They were not-- they were maybe the least wealthy of the family.
And they are the only whole family that survived in Russia.
All the family members-- the mother.
I mean, there are brothers, sisters, today even living.
And they survived.
And we-- you know, the ones who were left in Poland had very little chance.
So who should have known the ones that were sent to Siberia, they were going to be the lucky ones, compared to us.
And so this kept on going.
And my mother kept coming and said, oy, they almost caught me today.
Another they shoot.
Yeah, my mother tried.
She scrounged, she went.
I don't know what she did.
I don't remember.
Yes, we did.
Somehow they still had-- my mother maybe sold everything she had.
I don't know.
Maybe she still had-- whatever she had to sell, maybe she had to sell everything in order to just temporarily to-- you know.
She wrote letters to America.
She did everything she could as a woman.
I don't know how she did it.
I could never do this.
Believe me, I would have laid down and died, what she had to do.
She was the most amazing woman.
Because she had to care for us.
She had to make, I don't know, provide for us.
But she still had her parents.
And you know, it's easier when you have, you know, a mother, a father, brothers.
So somehow she scrounged, and she made the best she could.
So we lived like this for a year.
And then one day, I don't know, they decided, I don't know-- I just know that we went to Berezhany, which was more east, towards like Russia, in the direction of Russia.
But it was Ukraine, I believe.
It was a city, a pretty big city, Berezhany.
But not a city like Przemysl.
Przemysl was more historic.
Berezhany was-- looked like, I don't know, very small, low buildings.
And it had a different look.
A lot of Ukraine lived there, and some Jewish people came there.
We lived there with my grandparents-- my mother's parents lived there for a year.
And it was, again--
Yeah, all of them.
Everybody.
The whole family went there.
And so that I felt rich.
It was still normal for me.
A child doesn't feel the pain, what the grownups have to go through.
And I had my aunt, my uncles.
That's all I need.
I wanted my uncles, my aunts, my grandpa.
What did I need?
My parents-- you know, my mother, she did the best she could.
But it got harder.
And we didn't have much to eat.
And it were already-- It was so bad, I'll tell you how bad it was.
I remember that there was a lady, she had a-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] she had a goat nearby.
It was like sort of very in front of their houses, some people kept goats or chickens.
It was very sort of like a suburb.
It looked like suburb, even though it was the city.
Some people kept a goat, a chicken.
They laid the eggs, and the goat gave milk.
And the lady, I remember, my mother, when she peeled the apples or the potatoes, she told me to go to give the peelings to the lady.
And the lady would exchange it for a little milk for us.
That's the only way we got milk.
This is the beginning of the deprivation I start feeling.
I realized the situation we were.
And I remember that clearly.
Because every time she peeled it, she put it away.
And then, eventually, we saved it, and we gave it to that goat.
That goat ate the peelings from this.
And then, in exchange, she gave my mother a little milk for us.
So that's how she got her milk.
And so things got-- but I still went to-- the first time I started to go to a kindergarten.
So that part was nice.
I went with other children.
I started to learn Russian and songs and plays.
And I even remember I was supposed to appear in a play, and I was supposed to be a flower.
And I told my mother she should make me a special-- I should look like a certain-- I told her which flower I want to be.
And she said, yes, I'll make you.
Are you going to look like this and this flower?
Which flower was it?
Do you remember?
Yes, I do remember.
I think it was like a violet or something, a blue violet, a purple violet.
I don't know what made me say.
She said, I'll make you into a violet.
I'll make you should look like a violet.
And I was supposed to appear in the play.
And I learned how to sing songs and how to speak Russian there, because this was Russian run.
And I went myself.
I walked.
She taught me how to walk by myself.
All by myself, I felt so grown up.
You know, she didn't have to take me.
And it started again, normalcy.
And I started to know the town.
And I could walk to my grandparents a half an hour.
It was very nice, because, you know, I didn't need more.
A child doesn't need much more than their environment that they love and that's all.
You don't have to have a much food, but that's all you need is love and your own kind that love you, OK?
So that's how it was.
Then I was supposed to go to enter first grade.
My mother said that fall, I was going to go finally to school.
It's an exciting time for a child.
And I was going to learn how to read, for the first time how to read and be taught how-- My sister was already in school.
And she had her own book.
You had to buy your own book.
You weren't given a book.
And I remember, I looked at the book, and it was so exciting.
I was going to be also educated.
I mean, I was going to start reading, how to read.
And you weren't taught ABC.
You were taught how to start.
I remember the first word even, what it said in the book.
Adam, it said in the book.
And I kept looking so many times.
And my sister said, the next year, when you start going to school, you take my book, and I'm going to get from the next grade.
And I was very excited, even with a second-hand book.
And I was very excited.
And I was looking forward to becoming a child like other children, going to school, and having a normal life.
But it wasn't to be.
That very fall, when I was supposed to start the first grade, it was a very memorable time in my life, when you are about to go to enter school.
And you know what?
My life never-- even death I could not experience.
I lost it.
Because the Nazis, the Germans attacked Russia.
And we were under war.
One day I remember my mother was buying shoes for me to go to school.
I should go to school, new shoes.
And I was sitting in a shoe store, in the center of the so-called town, in a shoe store.
And all of a sudden, I saw bombs flying.
The bombs started.
They were attacked.
Russia was attacked.
That was-- everybody knows that.
And I saw this.
The bombs started to fly.
And everybody-- nobody knew this was going to happen.
Nobody was prepared for this.
Because Russia had some kind of a treaty with the Germans.
And there was peace for a year or so, two, I don't.
And then, all of a sudden, the whole thing really erupted, the war, full scale.
Because when the Germans marched to Poland, they took it in no time.
I didn't witness bombs.
There were no-- because they took it so easily, that there were no-- I didn't even hear a bomb where I live.
Maybe in the west.
But here I saw bombs in front of my eyes, falling.
It was a terrible thing to see and witness, to hear the bomb.
This is the most frightening thing you could ever hear.
It's like you think the world comes to the end when you see it, witness it.
From then on, it got worse.
The Germans-- What did you do?
How did you get home from the store?
We got home after they stopped.
I don't remember.
All I remember when they flew, the first time I heard a bomb in my life.
And from then on, it was chaos.
It was chaos, worse and worse.
It got worse, to the point that every day it got worse.
The Germans, once they entered the town-- first the bombs, then they entered.
And they occupied, and they've let the Jewish people feel that they are nothing.
I was once in the park.
I took my little sister to the park.
Yeah, I took my little sister by the hand.
My mother said, take your little sister Raizel to the park.
And it was a nice park.
Before the war erupted, I saw Russian soldiers dance the kazachok.
And I used to love to go there.
And they'd go up, down, and it was so beautiful.
I used to love to watch them dance, the Russian soldiers, you know, who occupied that park.
And I used to-- it was nice.
It was nice.
You could go for a walk.
And I loved to go to the park.
And that day, I didn't know there were people, Jewish people lined up like-- I don't know how to say this.
Like-- terrible.
Was a terrible thing, lined up in a row, as far as the eye could see.
And the Nazis were holding the sticks with those leather whips.
And they were whipping people.
And I saw this.
And when I saw this, all of a sudden I couldn't believe this.
I ran away, I walked away so quickly.
When I saw that scene, I realized how terrible, how things changed, all of a sudden.
A person was nothing.
It was like, you know, you were lower than an animal.
They were hitting people.
And people were just standing in a line, a line as far as the eye could see in the park.
Nothing.
I just took her home, quick.
And we went home as quick away from there as I-- I was afraid he was going to start whipping me too.
And I went away.
I didn't look like Jewish, but, you know, these people.
I don't know how they ever collected.
And this was one of the terrible scenes I remember.
The worst, one of the terrible scenes I ever saw.
It was whipping.
And I was so scared, and I ran and went home.
I don't remember that.
But it was terrible.
And from then, things got worse.
One day, my family-- my mother's family decided they're going back.
They had nothing maybe to eat or how to make.
So they went back to Przemysl, to the area where they were more familiar, their own surroundings.
They realized that they are in a strange place.
When you know a place-- when you know your surrounding, I suppose you can survive better.
Well, they didn't know where they were.
They didn't know any people that they knew there.
So they felt that maybe their chances of surviving maybe is better closer where they were born, or where they were familiar with.
So back to Przemysl we went.
Oh, no, I don't remember.
But I do remember clearly, it wasn't very long after we got back.
It was terrible.
One day, I don't remember whether it was a week or a few days, I can't remember that part.
But I remember-- you know, you remember the most terrible part, because it's such a terrible impression.
I remember one day, Nazis, I think they were Gestapo or something.
They came up, and everything-- they threw everything out.
And they start searching.
They tore the clothes apart.
They were searching for something.
They came.
They sort of broke into our apartment, where we stayed.
We didn't have much-- I remember, food was very scarce then.
We literally didn't have anything to eat anymore.
